content.text:
I miss a document that explains (in terms for advanced user / high-level dev) the sequence of the steps executed when running the pc-switcher. Is not in README and not in docs/, not even in the gold docs/system.
Maybe best in README.md, as that is really overview documentation for user "What happens when I run pc-switcher".

Very concise, just for me to understand and remember: which steps, in which order, are executed.
E.g. needed to be able to reason about how actions of one step influence the environment the next step runs in.
I'll dig into the actual execution flow before writing anything. Let me explore the orchestrator and related code.

input.description: List source files and README size

---README---
275 README.md

1	# PC-switcher
2	
3	A synchronization system for seamless switching between Linux desktop machines. Keep your laptops and workstations in sync with near-complete system-state replication.
4	
5	## Overview
6	
7	PC-switcher enables a simple workflow: work on one machine, sync before switching, resume on the other—without manual file management or cloud sync overhead.
8	
9	```plain
10	Work on source machine → Trigger sync → Resume on target machine
11	```
12	
13	**Status**: Core infrastructure complete. Core sync functionality in development.
14	
15	## What Gets Synced
16	
17	- **User data**: `/home`, `/root` with all documents, code, configs, and selective caches
18	- **Packages**: apt, snap, flatpak, PPAs, manual installs
19	- **Application configurations**: GNOME, cloud mounts, systemd services
20	- **System configurations**: Machine-independent `/etc` files, users, groups
21	- **File metadata**: Owner, permissions, ACLs, timestamps
22	- **Containers & VMs**: Docker images/containers/volumes, KVM/virt-manager VMs
23	- **k3s**: Local single-node cluster state and PVCs
24	
25	**Never synced**: SSH keys, Tailscale config, GPU/hardware caches, machine-specific packages
26	
27	## Installation
28	
29	Install using the installation script:
30	```bash
31	curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/flaksit/pc-switcher/refs/heads/main/install.sh | bash
32	```
33	
34	To install a specific version:
35	```bash
36	curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/flaksit/pc-switcher/refs/heads/main/install.sh | VERSION=0.2.0 bash
37	```
38	
39	Test installation:
40	```bash
41	pc-switcher --help
42	pc-switcher --version
43	```
44	
45	**Uninstalling**:
46	```bash
47	uv tool uninstall pcswitcher
48	```
49	(Note: Use the Python package name `pcswitcher`, not the command name `pc-switcher`)
50	
51	After installation, create the default configuration:
52	```bash
53	pc-switcher init
54	```
55	
56	## Quick Start
57	
58	Before syncing, ensure:
59	- Target machine is powered on and reachable via SSH (LAN, VPN, etc.)
60	- You're logged out from the desktop (or close all apps)
61	
62	Trigger a sync:
63	```bash
64	pc-switcher sync <target-hostname>
65	```
66	
67	Monitor sync progress with:
68	```bash
69	pc-switcher logs
70	```
71	
72	After sync completes, power off the source machine and resume work on target.
73	
74	## Configuration
75	
76	Run `pc-switcher init` to create the default configuration file at `~/.config/pc-switcher/config.yaml`, or create it manually:
77	
78	```yaml
79	# Logging configuration (see Logging Configuration section below for details)
80	logging:
81	  file: DEBUG      # Floor level for file output
82	  tui: INFO        # Floor level for terminal output
83	  external: WARNING  # Floor for third-party libraries
84	
85	# Sync jobs (true = enabled, false = disabled)
86	sync_jobs:
87	  dummy_success: true
88	  dummy_fail: false
89	
90	# Disk space monitoring
91	disk_space_monitor:
92	  preflight_minimum: "20%"  # Or absolute like "50GiB"
93	  runtime_minimum: "15%"    # CRITICAL abort if below
94	  warning_threshold: "25%"  # WARNING log if below
95	  check_interval: 30        # Seconds
96	
97	# Btrfs snapshots
98	btrfs_snapshots:
99	  subvolumes:
100	    - "@"
101	    - "@home"
102	  keep_recent: 3
103	  # max_age_days: 30  # Optional - enables age-based cleanup
104	```
105	
106	See default configuration in `src/pcswitcher/default-config.yaml`.
107	
108	## Logging Configuration
109	
110	Configure log levels in your `~/.config/pc-switcher/config.yaml`:
111	
112	```yaml
113	logging:
114	  file: DEBUG      # Log level floor for file output (default: DEBUG)
115	  tui: INFO        # Log level floor for TUI output (default: INFO)
116	  external: WARNING  # Log level floor for external libraries (default: WARNING)
117	```
118	
119	### Log Levels
120	
121	| Level | Value | Description |
122	|-------|-------|-------------|
123	| DEBUG | 10 | Internal diagnostics |
124	| FULL | 15 | Operational details (file-level sync info) |
125	| INFO | 20 | High-level operations (job start/complete) |
126	| WARNING | 30 | Unexpected but non-fatal conditions |
127	| ERROR | 40 | Recoverable errors |
128	| CRITICAL | 50 | Unrecoverable errors, sync must abort |
129	
130	### Default Behavior
131	
132	- `file: DEBUG` - All log levels written to file
133	- `tui: INFO` - Only INFO and above shown in terminal
134	- `external: WARNING` - External library logs (asyncssh, etc.) filtered to WARNING+
135	
136	Log files are written to `~/.local/share/pc-switcher/logs/` in JSON lines format.
137	
138	### Common Configurations
139	
140	**Debug SSH connection issues:**
141	```yaml
142	logging:
143	  file: DEBUG
144	  tui: INFO
145	  external: DEBUG  # Show asyncssh debug logs
146	```
147	
148	**Quiet mode (errors only):**
149	```yaml
150	logging:
151	  file: DEBUG     # Still log everything to file
152	  tui: ERROR      # Only show errors in terminal
153	  external: ERROR
154	```
155	
156	## Available Commands
157	
158	```bash
159	# Initialize configuration file
160	pc-switcher init [--force]    # Create default config at ~/.config/pc-switcher/config.yaml
161	
162	# Sync to target machine
163	pc-switcher sync <target-hostname> [--config PATH]
164	
165	# View logs
166	pc-switcher logs              # Show logs directory and list recent logs
167	pc-switcher logs --last       # Show path to most recent log file
168	
169	# Clean up old snapshots
170	pc-switcher cleanup-snapshots --older-than 7d [--dry-run]
171	
172	# Self-update pc-switcher
173	pc-switcher self update [VERSION] [--prerelease]
174	```
175	
176	## Requirements
177	
178	- Ubuntu 24.04 LTS on all machines
179	- Single btrfs filesystem (all synced data on one filesystem per machine)
180	- SSH access between machines (LAN, VPN such as Tailscale, or other network)
181	- Only one machine actively used at a time
182	
183	## Key Design Principles
184	
185	1. **Reliability**: No data loss, conflict detection, consistent state, full auditability
186	2. **Smooth UX**: Single command to launch entire sync; minimal manual intervention
187	3. **Use standard tools**: Well-supported, maintainable approach
188	4. **Minimize disk wear**: NVMe SSDs—avoid unnecessary writes
189	5. **Simplicity**: Easy to understand, modify, and maintain
190	
191	## Troubleshooting
192	
193	### GitHub API Rate Limits
194	
195	When running `pc-switcher --version`, `self update`, or sync (which installs pc-switcher on target), you may see rate limit errors like:
196	
197	```
198	RuntimeError: Failed to fetch GitHub releases: 403 {"message": "API rate limit exceeded..."}
199	```
200	
201	This happens because pc-switcher queries the GitHub API to check for releases. Unauthenticated requests are limited to 60/hour.
202	
203	**Solution**: Add a GitHub personal access token with public read-only permissions to your `~/.profile` on both source and target machines:
204	
205	```bash
206	echo 'export GITHUB_TOKEN=ghp_your_token_here' >> ~/.profile
207	source ~/.profile
208	```
209	
210	With a token, the rate limit increases to 5,000 requests/hour.
211	
212	## Documentation
213	
214	See [docs/README.md](docs/README.md) for the documentation index.
215	
216	Key documents:
217	- **[High level requirements](docs/planning/High%20level%20requirements.md)** - Project vision, scope, workflow
218	- **[Architecture](docs/system/architecture.md)** - System architecture and design
219	- **[Architecture Decision Records](docs/adr/_index.md)** - Design decisions and rationale
220	- **[Development Guide](docs/dev/development-guide.md)** - Development workflow and guidelines
221	
222	## Development
223	
224	Clone the repository:
225	```bash
226	git clone git@github.com:flaksit/pc-switcher.git
227	cd pc-switcher
228	```
229	
230	Install dependencies:
231	```bash
232	uv sync
233	```
234	
235	PR workflow:
236	- If a PR fixes an issue, include `Fixes #<issue>` (or `Closes` / `Resolves`) in the PR description/title so GitHub closes the issue on merge.
237	- See [docs/dev/development-guide.md](docs/dev/development-guide.md).
238	
239	Install the tool from your local checkout (for testing):
240	```bash
241	./install.sh
242	```
243	This auto-detects the git workspace and runs `uv tool install .` from your local code.
244	
245	If you want to install a specific version from GitHub, use one of the following commands:
246	```bash
247	# use the local script to install a specific package version from GitHub
248	VERSION=0.2.0 ./install.sh
249	./install.sh --ref abcd0123
250	./install.sh --ref my_feature_branch
251	# Use the install script from GitHub to install a specific package version
252	curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/flaksit/pc-switcher/refs/heads/main/install.sh | bash -s -- --ref my_feature_branch
253	```
254	
255	
256	Run code quality checks:
257	```bash
258	uv run ruff format .    # Format code
259	uv run ruff check .     # Lint
260	uv run basedpyright     # Type check
261	uv run pytest           # Run tests
262	```
263	
264	This project uses **SpecKit**—a specification-driven workflow via custom slash commands:
265	
266	```bash
267	/speckit.specify "feature description"  # Create feature spec
268	/speckit.clarify                        # Refine spec details
269	/speckit.plan                           # Generate design artifacts
270	/speckit.tasks                          # Create actionable tasks
271	/speckit.analyze                        # Check consistency specs-plan-tasks
272	/speckit.implement                      # Execute implementation
273	```
274	
275	See [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) for complete development workflow details.
276

content.text: I have the README. Now let me read the orchestrator and CLI sync flow to get the actual sequence.

result.output:
1	"""Command execution for local and remote machines."""
2	
3	from __future__ import annotations
4	
5	import asyncio
6	import shlex
7	from collections.abc import AsyncGenerator, AsyncIterator
8	from pathlib import Path
9	from typing import Protocol
10	
11	import asyncssh
12	
13	from pcswitcher.models import CommandResult
14	
15	__all__ = [
16	    "BashLoginRemoteExecutor",
17	    "Executor",
18	    "LocalExecutor",
19	    "LocalProcess",
20	    "Process",
21	    "RemoteExecutor",
22	    "RemoteProcess",
23	]
24	
25	
26	class Executor(Protocol):
27	    """Protocol for command execution on local or remote machines.
28	
29	    Both LocalExecutor and RemoteExecutor implement this protocol,
30	    allowing code to work with either without knowing which one it is.
31	    """
32	
33	    async def run_command(
34	        self,
35	        cmd: str,
36	        timeout: float | None = None,
37	    ) -> CommandResult:
38	        """Run a command and wait for completion."""
39	        ...
40	
41	    async def terminate_all_processes(self) -> None:
42	        """Terminate all tracked processes."""
43	        ...
44	
45	
46	class Process(Protocol):
47	    """Handle for a running process with streaming output.
48	
49	    Note: stdin is intentionally not supported. All commands must be
50	    non-interactive. This is a design constraint to ensure reliable
51	    automated execution without prompts or user input requirements.
52	    """
53	
54	    async def stdout(self) -> AsyncIterator[str]:
55	        """Iterate over stdout lines as they arrive."""
56	        ...
57	
58	    async def stderr(self) -> AsyncIterator[str]:
59	        """Iterate over stderr lines as they arrive."""
60	        ...
61	
62	    async def wait(self) -> CommandResult:
63	        """Wait for process to complete and return result."""
64	        ...
65	
66	    async def terminate(self) -> None:
67	        """Terminate the process."""
68	        ...
69	
70	
71	class LocalProcess:
72	    """Process wrapper for local asyncio subprocess."""
73	
74	    def __init__(self, proc: asyncio.subprocess.Process) -> None:
75	        self._proc = proc
76	
77	    async def stdout(self) -> AsyncIterator[str]:
78	        """Iterate over stdout lines as they arrive."""
79	        if self._proc.stdout is None:
80	            return
81	        async for line in self._proc.stdout:
82	            yield line.decode()
83	
84	    async def stderr(self) -> AsyncIterator[str]:
85	        """Iterate over stderr lines as they arrive."""
86	        if self._proc.stderr is None:
87	            return
88	        async for line in self._proc.stderr:
89	            yield line.decode()
90	
91	    async def wait(self) -> CommandResult:
92	        """Wait for process to complete and return result."""
93	        stdout_bytes, stderr_bytes = await self._proc.communicate()
94	        return CommandResult(
95	            exit_code=self._proc.returncode or 0,
96	            stdout=stdout_bytes.decode() if stdout_bytes else "",
97	            stderr=stderr_bytes.decode() if stderr_bytes else "",
98	        )
99	
100	    async def read_stdout_chunks(self, size: int = 4096) -> AsyncGenerator[bytes]:
101	        """Yield raw stdout bytes in chunks until EOF.
102	
103	        Use instead of the line-based `stdout()` iterator when the process
104	        writes carriage-return-delimited output (e.g. rsync `--info=progress2`)
105	        that would block a readline-based reader indefinitely (RESEARCH Pitfall 2).
106	
107	        Args:
108	            size: Number of bytes to read per chunk.
109	
110	        Yields:
111	            Raw bytes chunks as they arrive; stops at EOF.
112	        """
113	        if self._proc.stdout is None:
114	            return
115	        while True:
116	            chunk = await self._proc.stdout.read(size)
117	            if not chunk:
118	                break
119	            yield chunk
120	
121	    async def wait_result(self) -> CommandResult:
122	        """Wait for process exit and return the result, assuming stdout already consumed.
123	
124	        Reads stderr to completion (via the stderr pipe) and then waits for the
125	        process to exit.  The `stdout` field in the returned `CommandResult` is
126	        empty because stdout was already consumed by `read_stdout_chunks`.
127	
128	        Use this after draining stdout via `read_stdout_chunks` to obtain the
129	        exit code and any error output without calling `communicate()` (which
130	        would attempt to read stdout a second time).
131	        """
132	        stderr_bytes = b""
133	        if self._proc.stderr is not None:
134	            stderr_bytes = await self._proc.stderr.read()
135	        await self._proc.wait()
136	        return CommandResult(
137	            exit_code=self._proc.returncode or 0,
138	            stdout="",
139	            stderr=stderr_bytes.decode(errors="replace"),
140	        )
141	
142	    async def terminate(self) -> None:
143	        """Terminate the process."""
144	        self._proc.terminate()
145	        await self._proc.wait()
146	
147	
148	class LocalExecutor:
149	    """Executes commands on the source machine via async subprocess."""
150	
151	    def __init__(self) -> None:
152	        self._processes: list[asyncio.subprocess.Process] = []
153	
154	    async def run_command(
155	        self,
156	        cmd: str,
157	        timeout: float | None = None,
158	    ) -> CommandResult:
159	        """Run a command and wait for completion.
160	
161	        Args:
162	            cmd: Shell command to execute
163	            timeout: Optional timeout in seconds
164	
165	        Returns:
166	            CommandResult with exit code, stdout, and stderr
167	        """
168	        proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_shell(
169	            cmd,
170	            stdout=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
171	            stderr=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
172	        )
173	        try:
174	            stdout, stderr = await asyncio.wait_for(
175	                proc.communicate(),
176	                timeout=timeout,
177	            )
178	            return CommandResult(
179	                exit_code=proc.returncode or 0,
180	                stdout=stdout.decode() if stdout else "",
181	                stderr=stderr.decode() if stderr else "",
182	            )
183	        except TimeoutError:
184	            proc.terminate()
185	            await proc.wait()
186	            raise
187	
188	    async def start_process(self, cmd: str) -> LocalProcess:
189	        """Start a long-running process with streaming output.
190	
191	        Args:
192	            cmd: Shell command to execute
193	
194	        Returns:
195	            LocalProcess wrapper for the subprocess
196	        """
197	        proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_shell(
198	            cmd,
199	            stdout=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
200	            stderr=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
201	        )
202	        self._processes.append(proc)
203	        return LocalProcess(proc)
204	
205	    async def terminate_all_processes(self) -> None:
206	        """Terminate all tracked processes."""
207	        for proc in self._processes:
208	            if proc.returncode is None:  # Still running
209	                proc.terminate()
210	        # Wait for all to finish
211	        await asyncio.gather(
212	            *(proc.wait() for proc in self._processes if proc.returncode is None),
213	            return_exceptions=True,
214	        )
215	        self._processes.clear()
216	
217	
218	class RemoteProcess:
219	    """Process wrapper for SSH remote process."""
220	
221	    def __init__(self, proc: asyncssh.SSHClientProcess[str]) -> None:
222	        self._proc = proc
223	
224	    async def stdout(self) -> AsyncIterator[str]:
225	        """Iterate over stdout lines as they arrive."""
226	        async for line in self._proc.stdout:
227	            yield line
228	
229	    async def stderr(self) -> AsyncIterator[str]:
230	        """Iterate over stderr lines as they arrive."""
231	        async for line in self._proc.stderr:
232	            yield line
233	
234	    async def wait(self) -> CommandResult:
235	        """Wait for process to complete and return result."""
236	        await self._proc.wait()
237	        # Read remaining output after process completes
238	        stdout_data = await self._proc.stdout.read()
239	        stderr_data = await self._proc.stderr.read()
240	        return CommandResult(
241	            exit_code=self._proc.exit_status or 0,
242	            stdout=stdout_data,
243	            stderr=stderr_data,
244	        )
245	
246	    async def terminate(self) -> None:
247	        """Terminate the process."""
248	        self._proc.terminate()
249	        await self._proc.wait()
250	
251	
252	class RemoteExecutor:
253	    """Executes commands on target machine via SSH connection."""
254	
255	    def __init__(self, conn: asyncssh.SSHClientConnection) -> None:
256	        self._conn = conn
257	        self._processes: list[asyncssh.SSHClientProcess[str]] = []
258	        self._default_login_shell = False
259	
260	    def _wrap_for_login_shell(self, cmd: str) -> str:
261	        """Wrap command for execution in bash login shell.
262	
263	        Remote SSH commands run in non-login shells by default, which means
264	        ~/.profile isn't sourced and PATH may not include ~/.local/bin.
265	
266	        This wrapper ensures commands run with full user environment by
267	        wrapping them in 'bash -l -c "..."', which:
268	        - Sources /etc/profile and ~/.profile (login shell behavior)
269	        - Ensures PATH includes user-installed tools (uv, pc-switcher)
270	        - Simulates interactive SSH session environment
271	
272	        Args:
273	            cmd: Original shell command
274	
275	        Returns:
276	            Wrapped command with bash -l -c prefix and proper quoting
277	        """
278	        return f"bash -l -c {shlex.quote(cmd)}"
279	
280	    async def run_command(
281	        self,
282	        cmd: str,
283	        timeout: float | None = None,
284	        login_shell: bool | None = None,
285	    ) -> CommandResult:
286	        """Run a command on remote machine and wait for completion.
287	
288	        Args:
289	            cmd: Shell command to execute
290	            timeout: Optional timeout in seconds
291	            login_shell: If True, wrap command in 'bash -l -c' to source ~/.profile
292	                and ensure proper PATH. If None, uses the executor's default.
293	                Useful for commands requiring user-installed tools (e.g., uv, pc-switcher).
294	
295	        Returns:
296	            CommandResult with exit code, stdout, and stderr
297	
298	        Note:
299	            Using login_shell=True adds overhead (typically 10-50ms per command)
300	            due to profile sourcing. Only use when environment setup is needed.
301	        """
302	        use_login_shell = login_shell if login_shell is not None else self._default_login_shell
303	        if use_login_shell:
304	            cmd = self._wrap_for_login_shell(cmd)
305	
306	        try:
307	            result = await asyncio.wait_for(
308	                self._conn.run(cmd),
309	                timeout=timeout,
310	            )
311	            return CommandResult(
312	                exit_code=result.exit_status or 0,
313	                stdout=str(result.stdout) if result.stdout else "",
314	                stderr=str(result.stderr) if result.stderr else "",
315	            )
316	        except TimeoutError:
317	            raise
318	
319	    async def start_process(self, cmd: str, login_shell: bool | None = None) -> RemoteProcess:
320	        """Start a long-running process on remote machine.
321	
322	        Args:
323	            cmd: Shell command to execute
324	            login_shell: If True, wrap command in 'bash -l -c' to source ~/.profile
325	                and ensure proper PATH. If None, uses the executor's default.
326	                Useful for background processes requiring user-installed tools.
327	
328	        Returns:
329	            RemoteProcess wrapper for the SSH process
330	
331	        Note:
332	            Using login_shell=True adds startup overhead. Only use when
333	            environment setup is needed for the background process.
334	        """
335	        use_login_shell = login_shell if login_shell is not None else self._default_login_shell
336	        if use_login_shell:
337	            cmd = self._wrap_for_login_shell(cmd)
338	
339	        process = await self._conn.create_process(cmd)
340	        self._processes.append(process)
341	        return RemoteProcess(process)
342	
343	    async def terminate_all_processes(self) -> None:
344	        """Terminate all tracked remote processes."""
345	        for process in self._processes:
346	            process.terminate()
347	        # Wait for all to finish
348	        await asyncio.gather(
349	            *(proc.wait() for proc in self._processes),
350	            return_exceptions=True,
351	        )
352	        self._processes.clear()
353	
354	    async def send_file(self, local: Path, remote: str) -> None:
355	        """Copy a file from local machine to remote.
356	
357	        Args:
358	            local: Local file path
359	            remote: Remote destination path
360	        """
361	        async with self._conn.start_sftp_client() as sftp:
362	            await sftp.put(str(local), remote)
363	
364	    async def get_file(self, remote: str, local: Path) -> None:
365	        """Copy a file from remote machine to local.
366	
367	        Args:
368	            remote: Remote file path
369	            local: Local destination path
370	        """
371	        async with self._conn.start_sftp_client() as sftp:
372	            await sftp.get(remote, str(local))
373	
374	
375	class BashLoginRemoteExecutor(RemoteExecutor):
376	    """RemoteExecutor that runs all commands in bash login shell by default.
377	
378	    This executor wraps all commands in 'bash -l -c "..."' to ensure:
379	    - ~/.profile is sourced
380	    - PATH includes ~/.local/bin (for user-installed tools like uv, pc-switcher)
381	    - Environment matches interactive SSH sessions
382	
383	    Primarily used in integration tests where all commands interact with
384	    user-installed tools. Production code should use base RemoteExecutor
385	    and explicitly pass login_shell=True only when needed.
386	
387	    The login_shell parameter can still be overridden per-call:
388	        executor.run_command("sudo systemctl status", login_shell=False)
389	    """
390	
391	    def __init__(self, conn: asyncssh.SSHClientConnection) -> None:
392	        """Initialize with login shell enabled by default."""
393	        super().__init__(conn)
394	        self._default_login_shell = True
395

input.description: Read folder_sync job top and structure

result.output:
"""Generic folder sync job via rsync-over-SSH.

Syncs configured folders from source to target, running rsync as root on both
ends (local `sudo -E rsync` on source, `--rsync-path='sudo rsync'` on target)
to preserve cross-owner file metadata including POSIX ACLs and xattrs.

The key safety mechanism is the target-divergence guard in validate(): it uses
`btrfs subvolume find-new` to detect whether the target's synced subvolume was
independently modified since the last sync, aborting before the destructive
mirror+delete runs (D-06/D-07/D-08/D-18).
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import re
import shlex
from collections.abc import AsyncIterator
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any, ClassVar

from pcswitcher import sync_history
from pcswitcher.jobs.base import SyncJob
from pcswitcher.models import Host, LogLevel, ProgressUpdate, ValidationError

# Matches rsync --info=progress2 output, e.g.:
#   "9.53G 21% 317.26MB/s 0:00:28 (xfr#83063, to-chk=443926/538653)"
_PROGRESS2_RE = re.compile(r"\d+[\d.]*[KMGT]?\s+(\d+)%\s+\S+\s+\S+\s+\(xfr#(\d+),\s*to-chk=\d+/(\d+)\)")


@dataclass
class FolderEntry:
    """A single folder to sync with its include/exclude configuration.

    Excludes map to rsync `--filter=- <pattern>` arguments in order (first-match-wins).
    """

    path: str
    enabled: bool = True
    excludes: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

    def to_rsync_filter_args(self) -> list[str]:
        """Convert excludes to rsync --filter arguments.

        Returns a list of `--filter=- <pattern>` strings in config order.
        First-match-wins semantics are preserved because the order of the list
        is the order in which rsync evaluates the filter rules.
        """
        return [f"--filter=- {pattern}" for pattern in self.excludes]


class FolderSyncJob(SyncJob):
    """Generic folder sync via rsync-over-SSH.

    Syncs configured folders from source to target, running rsync as root on
    both ends to preserve cross-owner file metadata.  Enforces target-divergence
    check before mirror+delete to prevent data loss (D-06).

    Config shape (mirrors config-schema.yaml `folder_sync` section):
        folders:
          - path: /home
            enabled: true          # default
            excludes: [...]        # rsync filter patterns, optional
    """

    name: ClassVar[str] = "folder_sync"

    CONFIG_SCHEMA: ClassVar[dict[str, Any]] = {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "folders": {
                "type": "array",
                "items": {
                    "type": "object",
                    "properties": {
                        "path": {"type": "string"},
                        "enabled": {"type": "boolean", "default": True},
                        "excludes": {
                            "type": "array",
                            "items": {"type": "string"},
                            "default": [],
...

content.text: I have the full picture. Let me write a concise section into README.md, placed right after "Quick Start".

input.old_string:
After sync completes, power off the source machine and resume work on target.

## Configuration
input.new_string:
After sync completes, power off the source machine and resume work on target.

## What Happens During a Sync

`pc-switcher sync <target>` runs a fixed sequence of steps, orchestrated by
`Orchestrator.run()` (`src/pcswitcher/orchestrator.py`). The order matters: each
step sets up the environment the next one depends on. All steps run on the
**source** machine, acting on the **target** over a single SSH connection.

The sequence stops at the first failure (raising an exception), and the `finally`
cleanup always runs (release locks, kill remote processes, close the connection).

1. **Load config & arm interrupt handling** (CLI, before the orchestrator). Read
   `~/.config/pc-switcher/config.yaml`, start the asyncio loop, install the SIGINT
   handler that triggers graceful cleanup.
2. **Consecutive-sync check.** Abort (or warn interactively) if this machine was
   the *source* last time without a sync back — protects against overwriting the
   target's newer state. Skipped with `--allow-consecutive`.
3. **Acquire source lock.** Local lock file; this machine can now not join any
   other sync (as source or target).
4. **Establish SSH connection.** Creates the local and remote executors every
   later step uses. Nothing touches the target before this point.
5. **Acquire target lock.** A persistent remote process holds the same unified
   lock on the target; released during cleanup.
6. **Discover & validate jobs.** Load enabled jobs from config, validate their
   config, then run each job's `validate()` against live system state. This is
   where the **folder-sync divergence guard** runs: `btrfs subvolume find-new`
   detects if the target's synced subvolume changed since the last sync, aborting
   *before* any destructive mirror+delete. Nothing has been mutated yet.
7. **Disk-space preflight.** Check free space on both hosts in parallel against
   `preflight_minimum`; abort if either is short — so snapshots and rsync don't
   run a disk into ENOSPC.
8. **Pre-sync snapshots.** Create btrfs snapshots on both hosts. This is the
   rollback point; every mutating step below happens after it.
9. **Install/upgrade pc-switcher on target.** Ensures the target has a compatible
   version to run its side of later jobs. After snapshots, so a bad install is
   recoverable.
10. **Sync config to target.** Copy this machine's config to the target
    (prompting on diff unless `--yes`), so both ends run jobs with identical
    settings.
11. **Run sync jobs sequentially.** The actual data movement (e.g. `folder_sync`
    via rsync-over-SSH as root on both ends). A background disk-space monitor runs
    concurrently and aborts the sync if free space crosses `runtime_minimum`. First
    job failure stops the run.
12. **Post-sync snapshots.** Snapshot both hosts again, capturing the synced state.
13. **Record sync history.** On success, mark this machine `SOURCE` and the target
    `TARGET` (drives step 2 next time). Skipped in `--dry-run`.

With `--dry-run`, the workflow previews without writing state (no history update,
no mutations). `--allow-divergence` skips the step 6 guard after manual review.

## Configuration

The order matters: each step sets up the environment the next one depends on. All steps run on the **source** machine, acting on the **target** over a single SSH connection.

The sequence stops at the first failure (raising an exception), and the `finally` cleanup always runs (release locks, kill remote processes, close the connection).

content.text:
Done. I added a **"What Happens During a Sync"** section to `README.md`, right after Quick Start.

It's a 13-step numbered list mapping exactly to `Orchestrator.run()`, and it foregrounds the thing you asked for — how each step shapes the environment for the next:

- **Ordering rationale is called out** where it's load-bearing: divergence guard runs during validation *before* any mutation (step 6); disk preflight *before* snapshots/rsync (step 7); pre-sync snapshot is the rollback point that everything mutating comes after (step 8); target install after snapshots for recoverability (step 9); config synced to target before jobs so both ends agree (step 10).
- **Environment facts** noted: all steps run on source acting on target over one SSH connection; source lock then target lock; first-failure-stops semantics plus the always-run `finally` cleanup.
- **Flags** tied to the steps they affect (`--allow-consecutive` → step 2, `--allow-divergence` → step 6, `--dry-run` → no state writes).

I verified each claim against the source (`orchestrator.py`, `cli.py`, `jobs/folder_sync.py`) rather than the code's internal phase-number comments, which are slightly inconsistent with the actual call order.

One note: I referenced `src/pcswitcher/orchestrator.py` and `Orchestrator.run()` by name so the doc stays anchored to the code — if that flow gets refactored, this section is the thing to update alongside it.

{"body":"README.md says \n\n```md\n2. **Consecutive-sync check.** Abort (or warn interactively) if this machine was the *source* last time without a sync back — protects against overwriting the target's newer state. Skipped with `--allow-consecutive`.\n```\n\nThis is over-protective. It is totally valid to do A>B, continue working on A and then the A>B.  \nWe only want to protect against A>B, work on B, and again A>B.\n\nSo this warning/protection should only trigger if B was effectively changed between two consecutive A>B syncs.","labels":[],"number":159,"title":"Allow consecutive sync if target didn't change"}

content.text:
Labeled #159 with:

- **`type:feature`** — it's an enhancement to the consecutive-sync guard (make it condition on whether the target actually changed), not a defect.
- **`comp:core`** — the guard lives in the orchestrator's consecutive-sync check + sync history.

I deliberately left off:
- **Priority** (`P0`–`P2`) — you didn't indicate one, and I won't guess.
- **`ai:*`** — the change looks conceptually clear but has a real integration catch: the consecutive check currently runs *before* the SSH connection (step 2, pre-phase), whereas target-change detection needs the connection + btrfs `find-new` (step 6). That reordering pushes it past a trivial `ai:ready`, but I didn't want to unilaterally slap on `ai:needs-speckit`. Say the word if you want one added.

Didn't touch the issue otherwise, as requested.
